sentences.text: Yes.
Well, I could tell you a few more details.
The position got worse and worse.
As the German armies retreated, they evacuated more and more concentration camps, starting with Hungary, then all the camps around Vienna.
And all the camps, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], they all got pushed into Mauthausen.
They couldn't push them past Mauthausen because the rest was already-- Americans already came from the Western side, of-- from Germany.
So every-- all the camps were pushed into Mauthausen.
That means a steady supply of new people who had to walk all the way from Budapest to Vienna, from Vienna to Mauthausen, hundreds of kilometers.
So these people-- I was just lying-- I mean, I couldn't get up.
I was just lying on my bed.
And as the new people entered every day, couple of hundred, every second day they arrived, worn out from hundreds of miles of mar-- they were the strong ones.
And the weak ones, of course, got shot during the march.
These were the fittest of the fit.
And they were total wrecks.
They came in, big wounds everywhere, sore and bleeding, and beaten.
And they told me this.
That's how I knew what happened in Hungary, and about how they were driven all along the road.
There was no more transports to Auschwitz.
Auschwitz was already in Russian hands.
So there were no more railways to take them.
So they had to walk.
And on the way, beaten.
They were put in barracks.
And then put gasoline on them, and thousands of them got burned alive.

There were-- if there one man had typhoid, say, near the Hungarian-Austrian border, they put the whole regiment in, 2,000 people, lock the doors, put kerosene on it, and burned them, just because a few had typhoid.
Or there was a suspicion of typhoid.
That was enough.
These survivors came to tell their story.
So they came in.
They were relatively new in captivity, because they only left Budapest [INAUDIBLE] six weeks or eight weeks earlier.
And they died very quickly, because it was a total lack of food.
There was no food at all.
It wasn't like Auschwitz.
There no margarine.
No potato-peel soup.
There was hardly any food at all.
The position got-- got very much worse.
There was no more fresh bread delivered to the camp.
So they had to go to the storehouses where the bread was kept.
They went to the very bottom of it.
They had to dig out those breads-- thousands of loaves of bread-- which were there for six months or a year.
Now these breads were either stone-hard, deep-- deep-frozen, covered in mold, green, totally green, extremely hard.
And they dug out these very old breads, and they-- they gave that, a small portion of it, everybody got-- six or 10 people got one kilogram of this-- this mold-covered, rock-hard bread.
Now, under these conditions, was not surprising that more and more people had to be thrown out from the dead, the dead bodies from the beds.
But the man who was next to me, a son, about 20 year old, and a father about 45.
And the father died from hunger.
And the son wouldn't tell, wouldn't tell anyone that he is dead.
He just kept it a secret for himself.
And each time they came around with the soup, you know, soup, hot water with a bit of cabbage leaf, he would say, I'll take two, because my father is sleeping.
He wasn't sleeping.
He was dead.
So he had his double soup.
And they came around with this miserable piece of bread.
He would take two pieces of bread.
That went on here like for two or three days.
And that wasn't enough for him.
So, eventually, he got one of the knives, and he started to eat his father.
He just cut out a piece of wherever there was a bit of flesh to be.
He just peeled off a bit of flesh from him, and he was eating him slowly.
And then, this was all very fine.
But after a while, the father started to smell.
I mean, dead body.
I mean, it was very cold, so it wasn't too bad.
Wasn't like in hot weather, it would be worse.
[INAUDIBLE] was temperature inside was around zero.
Outside it was minus 20.
So he was eating his father slowly, but we told him that we don't really mind that he eats him, but we objected to the smell.
[INAUDIBLE] finished, whether you like it or not, we are going to report you, and we're going to throw out the body.
[INAUDIBLE] don't report me, just throw him out.
He's dead now.
He just died now, and, OK.
So we threw him out.
And the dead bodies were pulled outside.
At that stage, there were still a few men who were strong enough to pull out dead bodies from between the beds, hundreds of them every day.
But the time came when there was nobody strong enough to pull out the dead bodies.
And then came those last weeks when the dead bodies accumulated between the beds, in the beds, under the beds, between the beds and the toilet.
Well, actually, the toilets were overflowing, too.
It was jammed up.
So the excreta went over the dead bodies, and the-- the urination went, well, that was just a river between the-- the rows of beds.
So that was the general situation.
At this time, whenever I got my piece of bread, rock-hard, all green and yellow, you know.
I mean, the bread which is nine months old, and kept in the storehouse without air, and the water going through it, the snow and everything.
[CHUCKLES] It was a major operation.
I had to pull all my [LAUGHS] brain work, how I am going to eat this bread.
So I, with my spoon I-- I had plenty of time [INAUDIBLE] there was no hurry to eat this.
So it took me two hours to eat this little piece of bread, because I broke it down, not too-small pieces, [INAUDIBLE] lose them.
They're just manageable pieces.
And I had to warm it up in my mouth until it became soft.
And I had to eat it, one way or the other-- so, I-- with the mold and everything.
And I ate the bread.
But it was very painful.
It was touching my very sore gums.
Bleeding, they were bleeding, and pus, and everything.
So that was the situation there.
All these, that the people who were coming in from the transport, they told more and more horrible stories.
So we had a good general idea what was going on.
And the stage arrived when all you had the dead bodies inside.
Outside, the pyramids of dead bodies were already very, very high.
You couldn't build them up any higher.
Nobody could.
Both sides was full with dead bodies.
And we had the last SS paper, which was saying that the fights [INAUDIBLE] fights inside Berlin.
And then one-- one doctor, somehow, from the SS men, he got it, or he bought it, had a little radio.
And on the radio, we heard the news that Hitler committed suicide, and that the Americans are in Stuttgart, and in Munchen.
And the Russians took Vienna in April.
So we had high hopes.
But still, the problem-- we didn't see a German at that stage.
The SS men all disappeared.
They changed into peaceful farmers.
They just took off their uniform, put on a farmer's outfit, and attended to the lands in the neighborhood area.
And the Viennese police arrived, so there was no more cruelty.
Nobody hit anybody at that stage.
The problem was just there was no food.
That was a big problem.
The total-- total lack of food.
There was still 30,000, 40,000 people.
Most of them new arrivals, but I was the-- one of the few who were there for the previous four months.
I was there since January.
February, March, April-- well, not so much February.
Three months.
I was there three months.
And most people didn't last more than three or four weeks.
The sick people.
The healthy ones were sent away for slave work to the different tunnels.
So, anyhow, we didn't see any more Germans from then on.
We heard-- we heard the news that the Americans are advancing, but who knows when they get through [INAUDIBLE] the mountains is easy to defend.
So the SS were holding out [INAUDIBLE].
And at that stage, I became friendly to a Jewish boy who was-- my was 2 meter high up.
He was another meter, the highest level, fourth level, near the ceiling, he was.
He was lying like you would see where that lamp is up there.
I was a bit below.
So I only got one person's urination on my chest and face.
And the people on the bottom, they got three levels of urination.
But by that time, nobody could stand up.
We were-- we were just like skeletons, and almost dead.
And a lot of them died, of course.
But this boy was above me.
He was the same age as I was.
And we talked, in our way, that with a bit of luck, we should be liberated soon.
Maybe a few survivors will still be around.
Somebody to tell the story.
Well, our biggest worry was that everybody will die, and nobody will be, who can tell.
The SS men won't speak about it.
They are just peaceful farmers.
So we worried that the story will never come out.
That was our big worry.
And if the Americans would have arrived just three months later, there wouldn't be anybody alive, I can-- I can assure you.
There would have not been anybody alive at-- alive in that stage.
So anyway, I talked with this boy.
And then somebody said that he can hear gunfire.
And the gunfire became louder and louder.
And then somebody said on the radio that he can hear news that Salzburg has fallen to the Americans.
Salzburg is, you know, from Linz, and approaching Linz.
And we were not far from Linz.
We were about halfway between Salzburg and Vienna.
You know where they are, approximately.
And, you know, and I told him, look, with a bit of luck, if the American tank column is strong enough, they-- they might even get here this week, or they might even be here tomorrow.
And the gunfire was louder and louder.
And next morning-- it was about the 6th of May, or 5th of May, I think. 5th of May.
Next morning was a beautiful spring day, still fresh and [? right ?] around freezing point in the Austrian mountains.
And early in the morning, about 8:00, 9:00, I wake up from my deep coma, throughout the dead bodies around me.
And then I look out through the window, and I can hear some funny, heavy noise.
And I look out, and I see a row, about seven or eight American tanks [LAUGHS] going up on the mountain, approaching the entrance.
But we were outside the camp.
The main camp was on mountain.
We were to sick hospital.
Our camp was a little bit lower, so we could see the approaching the main camp.
The huge stars and stripes, the flag.
[SOBS] I have the same feeling as I tell the stories I had then.
I was crying from, you know, happiness.
And I say to my friend, look out, Peter.
Look out the window to the right.
American tanks are coming.
And I say it again.
He doesn't reply.
And then I say, is Peter up there?
And some other person, yes, he's up here, but he's dead.
He died that morning. 21-year-old boy.
That morning he died.
Anyhow, the American tanks went up.
The only thing what happened then, that the photo column, the-- the newspaper teams, or whatever.
There are no television men, but they had-- they had movie cameras.
They follow [? our ?] troops of the Americans eventually got to our barrack.
They couldn't come in.
The bodies were inside the beds, of course, between the bed.
They stood in the row, a big American soldier.
And he just yelled to his mate, look here, buddy.
I've never seen anything like it, he said.
And he kept filming like mad.
Says, look, I don't believe it.
Look at all the dead bodies everywhere.
And the other soldiers came, and they emptied their pockets out, just throwing chewing gum at us.
And then they threw in chocolate bars, American Hershey chocolate bars.
And then they had biscuits, chocolate biscuits.
And then somebody brought some tinned corned beef, which was absolutely useless.
First we couldn't open it, and if they could open it, and if you would eat it, you would die from it right away, anyhow.
So that was useless.
The chocolate was pretty useless.
Some of the [INAUDIBLE] were good, I suppose.
And I was very, very sick.
Very, very weak in all the excitement.
And the next day, a fleet of-- of Red Cross ambulances arrived.
But by that time, I was so weak-- I mean, even the previous weeks I was terribly weak.
I could hardly speak.
Just whisper.
And I certainly couldn't stand up, and I couldn't even lift up my arm.
I believe I was-- I was 32 kilograms.
From 72, I went down to 32.
It was pretty low.
So-- anbd my big worry was that they would shove me in with the other dead, because everybody was dead around me.
I was about-- 20 bodies, I was the only one with some life in it.
So I thought that they would just push me in with the others and throw me up to the truck.
So if I pass out, then I'm just lying there, everybody dead, why check this one?
So I was terribly worried.
So after the Americans came, and they kind of said, anybody alive here?
They were looking for people who are still alive.
And I said, I'm going to move my finger.
That's all.
I couldn't lift up my arm, no way.
Like, I could just do like that, so I trained my finger to move it like this to show some movement.
That's the only movement I could do.
Like that.

So I moved my finger, and eventually, sure enough, two big black-- black boys came along, and lifted me up, put me in an ambulance with a couple others, and we went down to the first village, where they evacuated.
The Americans were wonderful.
They thrown out the Germans from the high school, from the primary school, and all the public, the town hall.
[LAUGHS] This all-- all changed into hospitals.
They went from house to house.
They took out all the beds from the Austrian population.
They put beds into the town hall, into the hospital.
Hospital became a-- school became hospital.
They got all the sheets from the German homes.
They even arrested German young women.
You come with us.
You be nurses, and you're to take the chamber pots with you.
Bring your own chamber pot.
And when put in the hospital, I was amongst those people who maybe could be saved, possibly.
Not for sure, but a lot of us died still.
Heaps of-- I mean, the very first days, the people who opened up some of the food, they gobbled up the ham, or whatever the tinned food was, they died within hours.
I mean, stomach was just not used to eating such foods.
And so there we were.
And everybody was suffering from terrific diarrhea.
From whatever you ate, you had diarrhea.
So they called in teams of doctors.
Even before they give us-- and we were all naked, of course.
In the camp, in the barracks, we were naked, because everybody was full covered with excreta, so everybody threw away these striped uniforms.
So I was naked.
Eventually, I managed to get a singlet.
I managed to be-- I remember one little episode in the-- in this emergency hospital-- a school, I mean.
A hospital, it was.
And one day, I was getting strong enough to make a few steps to the toilet.
And I got out from my bed.
I had white sheets, and a bed-- a proper bed.
And I got out.
I only had the singlet on me, going down to my stomach.
And all these pretty German girls, who were all 20, 25-year-old girls.
And there was a-- an American Jewish doctor, a major or a captain, doctor who was in charge of the building.
And I'm going past him to the toilet.
And he said to me, maybe in German, that's probably a German Jew who migrated.
And he said to me, anyway, the message was that, aren't you ashamed of yourself, walking around with your private parts all showing?
And look at all these-- because he was in love with one of the-- what's his name?
Nurses.
So for him it was very embarrassing to see a naked man parading.
So I said, you give me a trouser, I'll put it on.
[LAUGHS] I haven't got one.
I got nothing.
Only thing I have is this singlet and this spoon.
That's all I have.
Sorry.
So, oh, well, I'll get you some trousers.
And they went out in a rage.
He was very upset about me.
I annoyed him greatly.
And eventually, came back and he brought me an underpant.
He couldn't get to trousers.
He brought me an underpant.
So next time you put this on when you go to the toilet.
I said, I will, I said, you know.
[LAUGHS] I'll do it.
Anyhow, from then on, they wonderful, the American Jewish officers, they were wonderful.
They organized food.
They established everybody's suffering from diarrhea.
So they got our-- I don't, I used to know the name-- special medicine which is available.
You need to take a spoonful every hour against to stop the diarrhea.
And then he organized food.
He organized special kind of-- not corn flake, but something similar, some porridge kind of thing, which-- which we got.
Only the mildest food cooked in water.
Not with milk.
So we got-- we got better and better food.
And then they established that I won't be able to survive it, so they picked me out.
They took me to another hospital where they specialized in blood transfusions.
And they specialized in giving glucose, blood sugar.
And then, I got massive treatment.
I was on a table, lying there, and getting all this red blood from, I don't know, maybe some American soldiers, or black soldiers gave it.
I don't know whose blood is in me, but I got every day blood transfusions to improve my blood.
And, of course, huge amounts of glucose [INAUDIBLE] and other stuff.
I don't know the exact terms.
Enough to say that when I was lying up there-- I'm jumping in time now.
This was already-- I'm just jumping forward to the-- to the middle of June.
A month later, I was lying on one of these tables, getting the blood transfusion.
I was still very skinny.
And a Hungarian boy who was not so sick as I was-- he only arrived to Mauthausen much later.
So he was in quite good condition.
And he said, I'm going back to Budapest.
There's a train next week which will take me back.
And I can give a message to your parents, if they are alive, assuming they are alive.
What is your message?
You tell them I'm here in the hospital.
And that's it.
I'll get back when I can stand up, and I can walk properly.
I get up.
And anyhow, the fact is, he got back to Budapest around early July.
That's two months after liberation.
And so he found my father.
He looked up his phone number, and he found him.
And he told him that, I met your son, and, you know, so and so.
He's your son.
He told me his  name and your name.
But I don't think-- I don't think he will make it here.
He won't come.
Don't-- don't count on it, because he was near death.
The doctors tried to give him many transfusions.
They're really doing everything they can, but a lot of people are dying still.
And I don't think he will make it.
And then my parents got really desperate.
And they bribed Russian officers.
They bribed-- are you interested in this part?
Yeah?
They did, they bribed Russian.
They gave vodka and whiskey to Russian officers, to secure a place on the truck, where they could go out to-- to Austria.
There are no trains going.
One military train once a week, or something.
And they try to search all the camps and [INAUDIBLE].
It was hard to say which camp I will be.
There's many hospitals.
All around every village there is three, four hospitals for the people who are recuperating.
So anyhow, they couldn't.
No Russian was willing to take them.
It was too dangerous, and they couldn't take them.
Anyhow, that's-- I still got a diary where I put down the first time I could eat a meal.
And the American brought a chicken noodle soup.
And I put it down in my diary, today I had my first proper chicken soup in 15 months.
I had it in March.
You know, March '44.
And I'll be talking about July, '45-- March, June, July-- yes, 15 months.
One and a quarter years.
And the soup.
I had the soup.
And then the American brought me some-- half a-- half a loaf of white bread.
And I was putting it in the chicken soup, and I was [LAUGHS] happiness.
I can't describe it.
And then the second, they brought me a piece of boiled beef.
Boiled beef with mashed potatoes.
Oh, God.
I wrote it down.
Today is my happiest day of my life.
I got boiled beef with mashed potatoes.
And so, I had a diary.
It goes on for about two or three weeks, about all the different food they brought.
Today we have stewed apples.
Things like that.
It's fantastic.
And so anyhow, I got slightly better.
And and I got better and better.
And I managed to-- managed to get up eventually.
You know, an American Jewish captain brought me the magazine.
And I said, bring me any back issues, if you can, because I don't know what happened in the world in the last year.
All I know is that Germany was beaten, but that's about all I know.
So he brought me magazine.
And I could spread the news about what's the news in the world.
Like, I could read it.
Others couldn't read English.
And was very good.
And eventually, the other interesting thing what happened is, that there was a train again.
Advertised for next week, there will be a train.
Just a-- what do you call it?
A cattle-- you know, cattle train, the same train which took us to Auschwitz.
But a train which will go from Mauthausen back to Budapest.
So all Hungarians, if you-- if you can walk to the train station, you can get on the train.
You're welcome.
The question is, can I walk to the train station?
So I had to walk, and I walked about a kilometer to the train station.
And it was on the train-- it's the same train that brought us, only they didn't put 100 people in, only 40.
And this train, very slowly, over three days and three nights, you know, the Russian army trains had first preference still.
So eventually we-- we got-- we got to the Hungarian border.
And the Russian troops were there.
And the Russians went through the train, and they took out all women to rape them.
They took out all women from 13 up till 73, and they raped them all.
But then they went through all the men.
And they checked any man who looks a bit prosperous, who has something in his pocket.
They took away all their possessions.
Then they come to me, and they found only one spoon in my pockets.
And Russians said, oh, tovarisch.
And he was sorry that I'm so poor, so he gave me half his bread.
So the Communists are good.
They helped the underdog.
The rich one, they took everything from them.
The more rich they were, the more they took from them.
But for me, they gave me bread.
So they gave me bread.
And then all the raped women came back.
And then the train could continue.
The journey took [? all. ?] Anybody who had a gold ring, he got badly beaten and punished, and the ring taken, of course.
And then, eventually, we arrived to Budapest.
[INAUDIBLE] tell you it is interesting part, when I arrived back, all I had is my striped Mauthausen uniform.
And the striped thing with the yellow star on it.
And-- and the train couldn't go, because all the bridges were blown up in Budapest.
Of course, you've got nine bridges.
They were all blown up by the Germans.
So the train stopped at an outside, outer suburb railway station, which was near Budapest.
Only six, seven kilometers away.
So that's it.
Everybody out.
So I got out.
And from there I had to find my way, without money, of course, without anything.
And I had my last little piece of bread also eaten up.
I had no food, even, and no money.
And I was a Jew, which was still not very popular with the Hungarian public.
The government was Jewish, but the public still hated the Jews, of course.
And then I had to find my way through a-- through a ship, a ferry service on the other side of the Danube.
And I remembered my nearest relative lived only three kilometers from here, and slowly, slowly, stopping every corner, sitting down in the gutter, because I was out of breath.
I was still very weak.
Maybe I was not 33, but 40 kilograms.
You know.
I could walk, but I needed a lot of rest.
Every five minutes, I'd stop, sit down 10 minutes, then go the five minutes.
Anyhow, by lunch time, from 6:00 AM until 12:00, in six hours, I covered all the distances.
And I arrived at this distant.
My cousin and his parents lived there.
And I just came in.
And they didn't know who I am.
[LAUGHS] They said I'm the son of Mr.
Dr.
[INAUDIBLE], the solicitor, you know.
Oh, oh, sit down here.
Say, are you hungry?
Yeah, I'm starving.
And then they went out, and I remember what they brought in.
They brought in two bread rolls, and they put butter on it.
Butter with a piece of salami.
And I eat this up for a start.
And they said, try to eat it slowly, because if you eat it fast, [INAUDIBLE].
So I ate up these two bread rolls with salami.
I had something to drink.
He said, now I'm going to go to the office of your barrister's quarters in another part of town.
He went up to him.
He didn't believe me.
He said don't make any jokes with me, you know, because we had the report that he's nearly dead.
But, no, he's still, he's not-- not very well, he's pretty sicklish, but he's here in our place.
And shall I continue?
It's very interesting now, anyhow, this part.
And my father came.
And he was there, and he was talking a bit.
[INAUDIBLE] And then the news came out, that while I was away, my brother, who was there in Budapest, he was murdered.
And I will tell you how.
Yes.
Can we stop here for a moment?
Well, I have either 10 or 15 minutes, or 20 minutes to go.
